title: Candle Wax on Linen
stainChemistry: Candle wax (paraffin) is a solid petroleum hydrocarbon that solidifies on linen as it cools, trapping any dye from colored candles within the wax matrix. The wax itself is not a stain — it's a physical deposit. However, dyed candles leave pigment behind after wax removal. The wax must be removed before the dye can be treated.
whyThisWorks: Freezing makes wax brittle on linen, allowing mechanical removal of the bulk deposit. Low heat (iron through paper towel) melts remaining wax into the absorbent paper by capillary action. POG dissolves any wax residue. If dye remains after wax removal, it's treated as a separate dye stain with appropriate chemistry.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Freeze with ice bag to harden wax. Scrape off bulk with dull edge
2. Apply POG to dissolve remaining wax residue
3. Apply NSD to lift pigment (for colored wax). Flush with cool water
4. If dye remains: apply H2O2 6% carefully, monitor
5. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) for pH restoration. Blot dry
warnings: Apply solvent BEFORE water — water spreads oil stains

steps: 1. Place ice in a bag on the wax for 10 minutes until brittle. Do NOT apply ice directly to fabric
2. Scrape off hardened wax with a dull knife or credit card edge
3. Place paper towels above and below the fabric (if possible). Iron on low heat to melt remaining wax into paper — check every 5 seconds
4. Apply Dawn dish soap to any remaining residue. Blot
5. Rinse with warm water. Air dry — inspect before using dryer
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 treatment rounds. Take to a professional dry cleaner — they have specialized solvents for oil-based stains
warnings: Always test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
Never add water to a fresh oil stain first — water and oil don't mix, you will spread it
Heat from dryer permanently sets oil residue

neverDo: No strong concentrated acids — weakens cellulose
No chlorine bleach on colored linen
fiberSensitivities: Strongest natural fiber when wet — very durable
Wrinkles easily but tolerates aggressive cleaning
Neutralize acid spotters after use on cellulose

escalation.when: If candle wax stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is candle wax on linen, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the oil fresh or has it been washed/dried already?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
